deliverables.hades:
## PE Mid-Market Acquisition Analysis — Mechanics & Modeling

### The LBO Model: Where Every Metric Lives

A PE analyst evaluating a mid-market target builds a leveraged buyout model. Everything else — the metrics, the benchmarks, the diligence — feeds into this structure. Here's how it's actually built:

```
LBO MODEL STRUCTURE

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  SOURCES & USES                                 │
│  ├── Purchase price (EV/EBITDA × Adjusted EBITDA)│
│  ├── Transaction fees (legal, advisory, D&D)    │
│  ├── Debt financing (senior, mezz, seller note) │
│  └── Equity contribution (the fund's check)     │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  OPERATING MODEL (5-year projection)            │
│  ├── Revenue build (bottoms-up or top-down)     │
│  ├── COGS / Gross margin                        │
│  ├── OpEx / SG&A                                │
│  ├── EBITDA                                     │
│  ├── Capex (maintenance vs. growth)             │
│  ├── Working capital changes                    │
│  └── Free cash flow to firm                     │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  DEBT SCHEDULE                                  │
│  ├── Opening balance per tranche                │
│  ├── Mandatory amortization                     │
│  ├── Cash sweep (excess FCF → debt paydown)     │
│  ├── Interest expense (fixed vs. floating)      │
│  └── Closing balance → feeds back to FCF        │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  RETURNS ANALYSIS                               │
│  ├── Exit EV (exit multiple × exit year EBITDA) │
│  ├── Net debt at exit                           │
│  ├── Equity value at exit                       │
│  ├── IRR (time-weighted return)                 │
│  ├── MOIC (money-on-money multiple)             │
│  └── Sensitivity tables (entry/exit multiple ×  │
│       EBITDA growth × leverage)                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

Every metric Poseidon lists maps to a specific cell range in this model. The model is the machine. The metrics are the gauges.

---

### Data Sources: What Feeds the Model

For a mid-market deal ($25M-$500M enterprise value), the data quality is fundamentally different from public companies. There are no SEC filings, no Bloomberg terminals pulling standardized data, no quarterly earnings transcripts. Here's what you actually work with:

| Source | What It Contains | Trust Level | Watch For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CIM (Confidential Information Memorandum)** | Seller's pitch — financial summary, growth story, market positioning | Low-Medium. Written by the sell-side banker to maximize price. | Revenue "hockey sticks" in projections. Cherry-picked metrics. Adjusted EBITDA that bears no resemblance to actual cash flow. |
| **Audited Financials (3 years)** | Income statement, balance sheet, cash flow statement, footnotes | Medium-High. Auditor opinion matters — qualified opinions are a flag. | Off-balance-sheet liabilities. Related-party transactions buried in footnotes. Changes in accounting policy between years. |
| **Management Presentations** | Forward-looking plans, pipeline, key customer relationships | Low. Management is selling themselves alongside the business. | Commitments without contracts. "Pipeline" that's actually just a wish list. Customer concentration they downplay. |
| **Quality of Earnings (QofE) Report** | Independent accountant's deep dive into true earnings power | High. This is the analyst's best friend. The QofE exists specifically to strip the CIM's spin. | If the seller resists a QofE, that's a red flag in itself. Differences between QofE-adjusted EBITDA and CIM-stated EBITDA >15% warrant hard questions. |
| **Tax Returns** | What the company actually reported to the IRS | High for revenue verification. | Discrepancies between book revenue and tax revenue. Personal expenses run through the business (common in founder-owned mid-market). |
| **Bank Statements** | Cash flow reality | Highest. Cash doesn't lie. | Timing of large deposits relative to period ends (stuffing). Unusual patterns (seasonal business being presented as steady). |

**The hierarchy of trust:** Bank statements > Tax returns > QofE > Audited financials > Management presentations > CIM. Work from the bottom up when something doesn't smell right.

---

### EBITDA Adjustments: Where Deals Are Won and Lost

Adjusted EBITDA is the single most important number in a mid-market deal. The purchase price is directly derived from it (EV = Adjusted EBITDA × Multiple). Every dollar of accepted adjustment at a 7x multiple moves the purchase price by $7.

**Legitimate adjustments (accept these):**

| Adjustment | What It Is | How to Calculate |
|---|---|---|
| **Owner compensation normalization** | Founder pays himself $800K/year; market replacement is $250K | Add back $550K. Verify replacement cost with comp data. |
| **One-time legal/litigation** | $200K lawsuit settlement last year, non-recurring | Add back $200K. Confirm it's truly one-time (check if there's a pattern). |
| **Non-recurring professional fees** | $150K for a system implementation, completed | Add back $150K. Confirm the project is done and won't repeat. |
| **Related-party rent normalization** | Owner charges company below-market rent for property they own | Adjust to market rate (this one goes BOTH directions — sometimes they overcharge). |
| **Non-cash stock comp** | Paper expense, not cash out the door | Add back. Standard and uncontroversial. |

**Aggressive adjustments (scrutinize hard):**

| Adjustment | Red Flag | How to Pressure-Test |
|---|---|---|
| **"Run-rate" revenue** | "We signed a contract last month worth $2M annually, so add $2M to revenue" | Demand the signed contract. Calculate only the portion that will flow through in the measurement period. Discount for churn probability. |
| **"Cost savings already underway"** | "We're cutting $500K in costs next quarter" | If the cuts haven't happened yet, they don't go in trailing EBITDA. Period. These belong in the operating model as upside, not in the purchase price as baseline. |
| **Pro-forma for acquisitions** | "We bought a company 6 months ago; here's what a full year would look like" | Only accept the actual results since close. Pro-forma full-year numbers assume integration is complete — it never is on day one. |
| **Customer "pipeline"** | "We have $5M in our pipeline that's about to close" | Pipeline is not revenue. It doesn't touch EBITDA until it's contracted and delivered. |
| **Excessive add-backs** | Total adjustments > 25-30% of reported EBITDA | The more adjustments, the less you're buying an actual business and the more you're buying a story. Aggregate add-backs exceeding 30% of reported EBITDA should trigger a pause in the process. |

**The mechanical test:** Take the seller's adjusted EBITDA. Subtract every adjustment you can't independently verify with a document (contract, invoice, bank statement). The number that remains is your **diligence-confirmed EBITDA**. That's what you underwrite to. The gap between seller's adjusted EBITDA and your confirmed EBITDA is your negotiating leverage.

---

### Working Capital Normalization

Mid-market sellers almost always try to extract working capital before close. This is how to catch it and normalize:

**Net Working Capital (NWC) = Current Assets - Current Liabilities**

But not all current assets and liabilities are operating. Strip out:
- Cash and cash equivalents (excluded — handled in enterprise value bridge)
- Current portion of long-term debt (excluded — handled in debt schedule)
- Income tax payable/receivable (excluded — normalized separately)

**What stays in NWC:**
- Accounts receivable
- Inventory
- Prepaid expenses
- Accounts payable
- Accrued expenses
- Deferred revenue (critical — see below)

**The NWC peg:** The purchase agreement will set a "target NWC" (usually trailing 12-month average). At close, if actual NWC is below the peg, the purchase price adjusts down dollar-for-dollar. If above, it adjusts up.

**Where sellers game this:**

1. **Delaying AP payments** before close to inflate current liabilities → lowers NWC → they extract cash. Watch for AP days outstanding spiking in the last 2-3 months before close.

2. **Accelerating AR collections** → lowers receivables → lowers NWC → they extract cash. Watch for DSO (Days Sales Outstanding) dropping unusually in the last quarter.

3. **Inventory drawdown** → lowers current assets → lowers NWC. If they're burning inventory without replacing it, you're buying a business that needs immediate re-stocking investment post-close.

**The calculation that matters:**

```
NWC Peg (Target):
  Average monthly NWC over trailing 12 months
  
  NWC(month) = (AR + Inventory + Prepaids) - (AP + Accruals + Deferred Rev)
  
  Target = Σ NWC(month 1..12) / 12

Closing NWC:
  Actual NWC on closing date balance sheet

Adjustment:
  If Closing NWC < Target → Seller owes buyer the difference
  If Closing NWC > Target → Buyer owes seller the difference
```

**Always use a 12-month average, not a point-in-time snapshot.** Point-in-time can be manipulated by timing a single large payment or collection. The 12-month average smooths seasonality and gamesmanship.

---

### Red Flags by Metric Category

Here's what triggers deeper diligence, organized by where it shows up in the model:

**Revenue Quality**
| Red Flag | What It Signals | Numerical Threshold |
|---|---|---|
| Top customer >25% of revenue | Concentration risk. Lose them, lose the business. | >20% = flag, >30% = serious concern |
| Revenue growing but cash collections flat | Booking revenue that isn't turning into cash. Possibly channel-stuffing or aggressive rev rec. | AR growing >1.5x revenue growth rate |
| High percentage of non-recurring revenue | One-time project revenue presented as recurring | <70% recurring in a "recurring revenue" business |
| Revenue declining but EBITDA growing | Cost cuts masking deterioration. Not sustainable. | 2+ consecutive years of revenue decline |

**Margin Quality**
| Red Flag | What It Signals | Numerical Threshold |
|---|---|---|
| Gross margin declining year-over-year | Pricing power erosion or input cost pressure | >200bps decline per year for 2+ years |
| EBITDA margin significantly above industry | Either genuinely exceptional or underinvesting | >2x industry median warrants deep dive into capex adequacy |
| SG&A as % of revenue dropping sharply | Cutting sales/marketing to inflate margins pre-sale | >500bps drop in the year before going to market |

**Cash Flow Quality**
| Red Flag | What It Signals | Numerical Threshold |
|---|---|---|
| EBITDA - Capex conversion <60% | Heavy capital requirements eating cash generation | <50% = capital-intensive business being pitched as asset-light |
| Cash flow from operations negative while EBITDA positive | Working capital consuming all the earnings | Negative CFO with positive EBITDA for 2+ years |
| Capex categorized mostly as "growth" vs. "maintenance" | Potentially understating true maintenance requirements | If "maintenance capex" < depreciation for 3+ years, the business is underinvesting |

**Leverage / Debt Capacity**
| Red Flag | What It Signals | Numerical Threshold |
|---|---|---|
| Net Debt / EBITDA > 4x at entry | Thin margin for error if performance dips | >4.5x for mid-market = aggressive. Lenders typically cap at 3.5-4x for this size. |
| DSCR < 1.2x | Barely covering debt service from cash flow | <1.1x = the business can't service its debt if anything goes wrong |
| Interest coverage (EBITDA/Interest) < 3x | Too much of earnings going to lenders | <2.5x = dangerous in a rising rate environment |
| Fixed charge coverage < 1.0x | Can't cover all fixed obligations from operating cash flow | <1.0x = structurally insolvent on a cash basis |

---

### The LBO Math: How Returns Are Actually Built

Three levers drive PE returns. Every metric ultimately maps to one of these:

```
LEVER 1: EBITDA GROWTH (operating improvement)
  Entry EBITDA: $10M
  Exit EBITDA:  $15M (50% growth over 5 years = ~8.4% CAGR)
  
LEVER 2: MULTIPLE EXPANSION (buy cheap, sell dear)
  Entry multiple: 7.0x
  Exit multiple:  8.5x (market re-rating, scale premium, strategic value)
  
LEVER 3: DEBT PAYDOWN (deleveraging)
  Entry debt: $35M (3.5x leverage)
  Exit debt:  $15M (paid down from free cash flow over hold period)

THE MATH:
  Entry EV:       7.0x × $10M = $70M
  Entry equity:   $70M - $35M debt = $35M (the fund's check)
  
  Exit EV:        8.5x × $15M = $127.5M
  Exit equity:    $127.5M - $15M remaining debt = $112.5M
  
  MOIC:           $112.5M / $35M = 3.2x
  IRR (5 years):  ~26%
  
RETURN ATTRIBUTION:
  From EBITDA growth:      ($15M - $10M) × 8.5x = $42.5M
  From multiple expansion: ($8.5x - $7.0x) × $10M = $15.0M  
  From deleveraging:       $35M - $15M = $20.0M
  Total value created:     $77.5M ✓ ($112.5M - $35M)
```

**Mid-market benchmarks for acceptable returns:**

| Metric | Floor | Target | Strong |
|---|---|---|---|
| **IRR** | 20% | 25-30% | >30% |
| **MOIC** | 2.0x | 2.5-3.5x | >3.5x |
| **Hold period** | 3 years | 4-5 years | <4 years at >2.5x |
| **Cash-on-cash yield** | 0% (all growth) | 5-10% annual distributions | >10% (dividend recaps) |

**The sensitivity table every analyst builds:**

```
                    Exit EBITDA Multiple
                 6.0x   7.0x   8.0x   9.0x   10.0x
Exit         $12M │ 1.4x  1.8x   2.2x   2.5x   2.9x
EBITDA       $14M │ 1.8x  2.3x   2.7x   3.2x   3.6x
             $16M │ 2.2x  2.7x   3.3x   3.8x   4.3x
             $18M │ 2.6x  3.2x   3.8x   4.4x   5.0x
             $20M │ 3.0x  3.7x   4.3x   5.0x   5.7x
             
             (MOIC values; entry at 7.0x × $10M, $35M equity)
```

This table is the decision engine. If the deal only works in the top-right corner (requires both significant EBITDA growth AND multiple expansion), it's fragile. If it works in the bottom-left (even with flat EBITDA and multiple compression, you still get 2x+), it's resilient. **Always underwrite to the bottom-left. Hope for the top-right.**

---

### Technical Gotchas Specific to Mid-Market

**1. Audited ≠ Accurate at this size.** Mid-market companies often use regional accounting firms, not Big 4. Audit quality varies enormously. Always cross-reference audited financials against tax returns and bank statements. Discrepancies >5% on revenue or >10% on net income warrant a conversation with the auditors directly.

**2. Owner perks are everywhere.** Personal vehicles, family members on payroll, personal travel expensed to the business, country club memberships, personal insurance. These are legitimate add-backs but must be identified exhaustively. The QofE report should catch them, but ask management directly: "Walk me through every personal expense that flows through the P&L." They'll volunteer the car and the club. They'll forget about the wife's salary.

**3. Deferred revenue is a liability, not an asset.** If the business collects annual subscriptions upfront, deferred revenue on the balance sheet represents an obligation to deliver service. When you acquire the business, you inherit that obligation. This means the cash has already been spent but the work hasn't been done. Deferred revenue must be included in NWC calculations and understood as a future cost, not free money.

**4. Tax structure changes everything.** Most mid-market businesses are S-corps or LLCs (pass-through entities). Their reported taxes are meaningless because the owners pay personally. You must **tax-affect** the earnings to model what the business would look like as a C-corp post-acquisition (since PE typically converts to C-corp or holds through a C-corp blocker). Standard approach: apply a 25% synthetic tax rate to pre-tax income to get pro-forma net income.

**5. Capex timing tricks.** A seller might defer maintenance capex in the year before sale to inflate FCF. Check capex as % of revenue over the last 3-5 years. If it's been steady at 5% and drops to 2% in the trailing year, the business is being starved. You'll spend that capex in year one post-close — model it.

That's the mechanical layer. Every metric Poseidon names has a calculation path, a data source, a manipulation vector, and a red flag threshold. The model is where it all converges. Build it from the bottom-left of the sensitivity table, and you'll know what you're actually buying.

# Key Financial Metrics for Mid-Market PE Acquisition Analysis
*B3C Council — Zeus ⚡ · Poseidon 🔱 · Hades 🔨*

---

## The Question a PE Analyst Is Actually Answering

Before the metrics: a PE analyst is not doing accounting. They are answering one question with two parts — **can this business generate returns above what we paid for it, and is there a credible path to realizing those returns?**

Every metric below is a proxy for some dimension of that question. But before running a single number, one distinction must be clear:

**Business quality** is what the underlying company actually is. Does it have a durable right to generate cash? The metrics that reveal this are operating-side: gross margin stability, revenue retention, working capital behavior, maintenance capex requirements.

**Deal quality** is what you're getting for your money. Is the price right, is the structure survivable, is there a realistic exit? The metrics that reveal this are valuation multiples relative to peers, leverage ratios relative to cash generation, and exit assumptions relative to where comparable businesses have actually traded.

A great business at a bad price is a bad deal. A mediocre business at a great price can be a good deal. The analysis fails when analysts conflate these two things — typically by letting a clean set of metrics do the work that judgment should be doing.

---

## The Metric Stack: Organized by Category

Mid-market PE ($5-50M EBITDA target) operates in a different regime than large-cap. Entry multiples are 6-10x EV/EBITDA (vs. 12x+ large-cap). Leverage tops out at 3.5-4.5x (vs. 6x). The businesses are usually founder-owned, EBITDA is routinely gamed through add-backs, and exits are typically strategic acquirers or up-market PE funds — not IPOs.

### I. Valuation Multiples

| Metric | Mid-Market Benchmark | What It Reveals | Key Watch |
|---|---|---|---|
| **EV/EBITDA** | 6-10x (Tech 9x, Industrial 7x, Services 6x) | Entry price relative to earnings capacity | Add-backs >20% of reported EBITDA inflate this artificially |
| **EV/Revenue** | 1-3x | Useful for recurring-revenue or SaaS-like businesses where EBITDA is depressed by growth investment | One-time revenue >15% of total makes this unreliable |
| **EV/EBITDA - Capex** | Tighter than EV/EBITDA | Adjusts for capital intensity — important when comparing asset-light vs. asset-heavy businesses | Capex being understated makes this misleadingly attractive |

*Note on P/E: Price-to-earnings is rarely the operative metric in mid-market PE analysis. Most targets are S-corps or LLCs with pass-through tax structures — their reported earnings are meaningless without tax-affecting. Use EV/EBITDA and EV/Revenue as your primary valuation anchors; tax-affect to pro-forma net income (25% synthetic rate) only for secondary checks.*

### II. Returns Analysis

| Metric | Target | Floor | Strong |
|---|---|---|---|
| **IRR (5-year hold)** | 25-30% | 20% | >30% |
| **MOIC** | 2.5-3.5x | 2.0x | >3.5x |
| **Cash-on-cash yield** | 5-10% annual | 0% | >10% (dividend recaps) |
| **Hold period** | 4-5 years | 3 years | <4 years at >2.5x |

Returns in PE are built by three levers — and understanding which lever is driving your model matters:

```
LEVER 1: EBITDA GROWTH (operating improvement)
  Entry EBITDA $10M → Exit EBITDA $15M (8.4% CAGR over 5 years)

LEVER 2: MULTIPLE EXPANSION (buy cheap, sell dear)
  Entry multiple 7.0x → Exit multiple 8.5x

LEVER 3: DEBT PAYDOWN (deleveraging)
  Entry debt $35M → Exit debt $15M (FCF cash sweep over hold)

THE MATH:
  Entry EV: 7.0x × $10M = $70M
  Entry equity: $70M - $35M = $35M (the fund's check)
  
  Exit EV: 8.5x × $15M = $127.5M
  Exit equity: $127.5M - $15M = $112.5M
  
  MOIC: $112.5M / $35M = 3.2x | IRR (5 years): ~26%

RETURN ATTRIBUTION:
  EBITDA growth:      ($15M - $10M) × 8.5x = $42.5M
  Multiple expansion: ($8.5x - $7.0x) × $10M = $15.0M
  Deleveraging:       $35M - $15M = $20.0M
  Total: $77.5M ✓
```

The deal is fragile if it only works in the top-right corner of the sensitivity table (requires both EBITDA growth AND multiple expansion). It's resilient if it works in the bottom-left. **Always underwrite to the bottom-left. Hope for the top-right.**

```
                    Exit EBITDA Multiple
                 6.0x   7.0x   8.0x   9.0x   10.0x
Exit         $12M │ 1.4x   1.8x   2.2x   2.5x   2.9x
EBITDA       $14M │ 1.8x   2.3x   2.7x   3.2x   3.6x
             $16M │ 2.2x   2.7x   3.3x   3.8x   4.3x
             $18M │ 2.6x   3.2x   3.8x   4.4x   5.0x
             $20M │ 3.0x   3.7x   4.3x   5.0x   5.7x
```

### III. Leverage and Debt Capacity

| Metric | Mid-Market Benchmark | Red Flag |
|---|---|---|
| **Net Debt / EBITDA** | <4x at entry (lenders typically cap at 3.5-4x) | >4.5x = aggressive; thin margin if performance dips |
| **DSCR (Debt Service Coverage)** | >1.5x | <1.2x = barely covering service; <1.1x = structurally at risk |
| **Interest Coverage (EBITDA / Interest)** | >2x | <2.5x in a rising rate environment = dangerous |
| **Fixed Charge Coverage** | >1.2x | <1.0x = can't cover fixed obligations from operating cash flow |

Floating rate debt exposure deserves specific attention at current rates (~4-5%). Model both current rate and +200bps stress scenario. Mid-market LBO debt is commonly L+400-500bps on senior; check whether your DSCR holds under rate stress before committing to the structure.

### IV. Operating Performance

| Metric | Mid-Market Benchmark | What Structural Cash Generation Looks Like |
|---|---|---|
| **EBITDA Margin** | 15-25% | Stable or expanding across economic cycles, not inflated by deferred reinvestment |
| **Gross Margin** | 40-60% (varies widely by sector) | Stable or improving; reflects pricing power |
| **Revenue Growth (3yr CAGR)** | >10% | Organic growth only — back out acquisition-driven growth to see the true organic rate |
| **Revenue Retention / Churn** | >85% retention (<15% churn) | The single most predictive metric for recurring revenue businesses |
| **DSO (Days Sales Outstanding)** | <60 days | AR aging >30% in the 90+ day bucket = revenue quality problem |
| **Inventory Turnover** | >4x | <4x suggests obsolescence risk or demand weakness |
| **Maintenance Capex / Revenue** | <5% (asset-light); higher for industrials | If maintenance capex < depreciation for 3+ consecutive years, the business is being starved |
| **FCF Conversion (EBITDA - Capex)** | >60% | <50% = capital-intensive business being pitched as asset-light |

The core operating question behind every metric: **is this cash generation structural or situational?** EBITDA can look excellent because of pricing power and operational discipline (structural) or because the owner deferred capex, cut sales staff, and stretched payables before sale (situational and non-recurring). Only one of these survives under new ownership.

### V. Quality of Earnings Indicators

| Metric | What You're Testing | Red Flag |
|---|---|---|
| **Add-backs as % of reported EBITDA** | Normalized earnings power | >25% = buying a story, not a business; >30% = pause the process |
| **Recurring revenue %** | Revenue durability | <70% in a business presented as "recurring revenue" |
| **Customer concentration** | Key account risk | Top customer >20% = flag; >30% = serious concern |
| **Related-party revenue** | Artificial revenue | >10% of revenue from related parties |
| **QofE vs. CIM EBITDA gap** | How much spin was in the pitch | Gap >15% = material misrepresentation risk |

---

## The Data Trust Hierarchy

Mid-market targets don't have SEC filings or Bloomberg data. What you actually work with, in descending order of reliability:

```
BANK STATEMENTS       ← Highest. Cash doesn't lie.
TAX RETURNS           ← High. IRS-reported numbers are harder to inflate.
QOFE REPORT           ← High. Independent accountant stripping the CIM's spin.
AUDITED FINANCIALS    ← Medium-High. Quality varies — regional firms ≠ Big 4.
MANAGEMENT PRESENTATIONS  ← Low. They're selling themselves alongside the business.
CIM                   ← Lowest. Written by the sell-side banker to maximize price.
```

When something doesn't reconcile, work bottom-up. If bank statements and tax returns diverge from the audited financials, the auditors are wrong or the presentation is aggressive — not the bank. If the seller resists providing a QofE, treat that resistance itself as a red flag.

---

## Where Sellers Game the Numbers: Mid-Market Specifics

**EBITDA add-backs** are the primary battlefield. Mid-market owner-operators routinely run personal aircraft, family members on payroll, country club memberships, personal insurance, and personal travel through the business. These are legitimate add-backs — but they must be identified exhaustively. The QofE will catch most of them. Ask management directly: *"Walk me through every personal expense flowing through the P&L."* They'll volunteer the car and the club. They'll forget about the spouse's salary.

**The mechanical test:** Take the seller's adjusted EBITDA. Subtract every add-back you cannot independently verify with a document (signed contract, paid invoice, bank statement). What remains is your diligence-confirmed EBITDA. The gap between the seller's number and yours is your negotiating leverage on price.

**Working capital compression** is the other common pre-sale manipulation: stretching AP, accelerating AR collections, and running down inventory to make cash flow look better than it structurally is. When normalized operations resume post-close, working capital expands and consumes cash you didn't model.

The NWC peg structure protects you: the purchase agreement should set target NWC as the trailing 12-month average (not a point-in-time snapshot, which can be manipulated). If closing NWC falls below the peg, the purchase price adjusts down dollar-for-dollar.

**Deferred revenue is a liability, not an asset.** If the business collects annual subscriptions upfront, the balance sheet deferred revenue represents a service obligation you inherit at close. The cash is already gone. It must be included in NWC calculations and modeled as a future cost.

**Tax structure requires adjustment.** Most mid-market businesses are S-corps or LLCs. Reported taxes are meaningless — owners pay personally. Tax-affect earnings at 25% synthetic rate to model the pro-forma C-corp economics you'll actually own.

**Capex timing.** If historical capex runs at 5% of revenue and drops to 2% in the trailing year, the business is being starved. You'll spend that deferred maintenance in year one. Model it.

---

## Exit Is Half the Thesis

Returns are created by the spread between entry and exit multiple, multiplied by value built in between. The exit multiple assumption is not a given — it is a hypothesis about what a future buyer will pay for this business, in this sector, at that future point in time.

Stress-test that hypothesis against actual transaction history: who has paid what multiple for comparable businesses, under what market conditions, and what drove outliers. Mid-market strategic exits average 8-12x; PE-to-PE up-market exits average 6-8x. If your return thesis requires a 10x exit in a sector where recent precedents show 7-8x, you are building on an assumption, not an analysis.

---

## One-Page Summary: What to Track and Why

| Category | Top Metrics | What They Reveal |
|---|---|---|
| **Valuation** | EV/EBITDA, EV/Revenue | Entry price vs. peers; whether you're paying for a business or a story |
| **Returns** | IRR, MOIC, sensitivity table | Whether the deal works under realistic and stressed scenarios |
| **Leverage** | Net Debt/EBITDA, DSCR, interest coverage | Whether the debt structure is survivable |
| **Operating** | Gross margin, EBITDA margin, revenue retention, DSO | Whether cash generation is structural or situational |
| **Cash Flow** | FCF conversion, NWC as % of revenue, maintenance capex | Whether EBITDA actually becomes cash |
| **QofE** | Add-backs %, recurring revenue %, customer concentration | Whether the numbers reflect economic reality or seller optimization |

The metrics are the instrument panel. The QofE is the mechanic's inspection. The sensitivity table is the stress test. Use all three, in that order. Never let clean metrics substitute for judgment about what the numbers actually mean.
